title: There Is Certainly No Place for Corruption in Justice!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. All seven source pages were checked for the judicial chronology, judges' names, sentences and fines, the ten-item loan table and both competing totals, press quotations, hearing lists, and signature date. Clearly visible OCR character errors and accidental word breaks were corrected only in the reading copy. Reports and suspicions mentioned by the letter are translated as attributed reports, not recast as independent findings.
paragraphs: On 8 January 2016, reports appeared under these headlines: “Judge Changed in Case Against Jayalalithaa” in *Dinamalar*; “Judge Changed in Jayalalithaa Disproportionate-Assets Case” in the Tamil edition of *The Hindu*; “Judge Hearing Disproportionate-Assets Appeal Changed” in *Daily Thanthi*; “Sudden Change of Judge in Jayalalithaa Assets Case in Supreme Court” in *Dinakaran*; and “Assets Case: New Judge on SC Bench” in the English edition of *The Hindu*.

Those reports specifically said that one of the judges hearing the appeal in Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case had been replaced. Justice Pinaki Chandra Ghose had initially been appointed the senior judge on the Bench, assisted by Justice Agrawal. When the case was last heard on 23 November 2015, it was stated that 8 January would be fixed for the final arguments and that the hearing would proceed continuously on every working day thereafter.
Let me briefly recall the history of this case.

The Tamil Nadu Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption registered a case in 1996 alleging that, while Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu between 1991 and 1996, Jayalalithaa amassed assets worth ₹66 crore beyond her known income. For the past nineteen years, that case has travelled through various courts and is now before the Supreme Court.

After conducting the trial, Special Court Judge John Michael D'Cunha in Bengaluru delivered judgment on 27 September 2014. He sentenced Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi to four years' imprisonment each, imposed a fine of ₹100 crore and ₹1 lakh on Jayalalithaa, and fines of ₹10 crore and ₹10,000 on each of the other three. All four, including Jayalalithaa, were then imprisoned.

Jayalalithaa sought release on bail. The matter went to the Supreme Court, where Chief Justice Dattu granted her bail on 17 November 2014. He also directed the Karnataka High Court to hear and decide her appeal within three months. Strong criticism of both that decision and Chief Justice Dattu appeared in the press at the time.

Jayalalithaa's appeal was then heard by a Special Bench of the Karnataka High Court from 5 January to 11 March. During the hearing, Justice Kumaraswamy made several observations in open court to counsel appearing for the accused, and the newspapers continually reported them. He said in particular that their arguments had produced no evidence, had failed to untie more than 150 knots in Judge D'Cunha's judgment, and had not earned adequate marks. He openly observed that he was in a position where he might have to deliver the same judgment as D'Cunha.
Meanwhile, a Supreme Court Constitution Bench comprising Justices Dipak Misra, R.K. Agrawal and Prafulla C. Pant, referring in a judgment to the forthcoming Karnataka High Court decision, observed:

> “The High Court judge must consider the gravity of the charges against the accused. The High Court must remain conscious of how corruption is eating away at society like termites. It must comprehensively and collectively assess the evidence before the Court and deliver judgment. A judgment rendered without likes or dislikes must set out its conclusions firmly and without bias.”
Supreme Court Justice Madan B. Lokur went further:
> “This appeal is an example of how a criminal case should not be conducted. The system of delivering justice in criminal cases has failed. It must be corrected. The time for that has arrived.”
Why did the Supreme Court Bench make such unusual observations?

A few days before the High Court judgment, reports circulated that the Chief Justice of India himself had come to Bengaluru and that various efforts were under way to secure a judgment favourable to the accused Jayalalithaa.

On 11 May 2015, Karnataka High Court Justice Kumaraswamy acquitted Jayalalithaa in the case. AIADMK members celebrated it as a victory, and Jayalalithaa issued a statement saying justice had triumphed. For our part, we wondered why there was such a great difference between the language Justice Kumaraswamy had used while conducting the hearing and his eventual judgment.
The very next day, many people pointed to enormous errors in the judgment, errors substantial enough to reverse its result.

Justice Kumaraswamy treated various bank loans obtained by the Jayalalithaa side as income and included in his judgment a list of loans they had obtained from Indian Bank, together with their total. Set out in detail as the judge recorded them, the list was:

- Loan to Jaya Publications: ₹1,50,00,000 - Loan transferred from Gunabhushani: ₹3,75,00,000 - Loan to Jayalalithaa: ₹90,00,000 - Loan to J. Real Estate: ₹25,00,000 - Loan to J.S. Housing: ₹12,46,000 - Loan to J. Farm House: ₹50,00,000 - Loan to Sasikala: ₹25,00,000 - Loan to V.N. Sudhakaran: ₹1,57,00,000 - Loan to Ramaraj Agro Mills: ₹1,65,00,000 - Loan for Mahalakshmi Marriage Hall: ₹17,85,274
Justice Kumaraswamy stated in his judgment that the total loans obtained by the Jayalalithaa side amounted to ₹24,17,31,274.

But ask anyone who knows addition to total those ten loans, and the result is only ₹10,67,31,274. Yet the Karnataka High Court judge said in his judgment that their total was ₹24,17,31,274. On the basis of that total, he reasoned that it had been wrong to prosecute Jayalalithaa for accumulating assets worth ₹66 crore and acquitted her.

That was not all. Many people explained in detail that the existing law contained no rule saying there was no punishment when disproportionate assets remained within ten per cent of income, and that no such provision appeared in the amendment made to the law in 1988 when Rajiv Gandhi was Prime Minister.
Pointing to these matters, many argued that the judgment contained a Himalayan error, that it must be reversed, and that the Karnataka Government itself must appeal to the Supreme Court in this case.

On 22 May 2015, *The Times of India* examined Justice Kumaraswamy's judgment in considerable detail under the headlines, “Curious Case of Amma Assets: Overlooking the Obvious” and “From Calculation to Take on Gifts, Karnataka High Court Order Has Many Loopholes.” It said the Karnataka High Court's 919-page judgment contained many unusual statements and conclusions, and explained nine irregularities with supporting figures.
On 23 May 2015, *Theekkathir* wrote:

> “Although Jayalalithaa has been acquitted in the disproportionate-assets case, that acquittal was obtained only because the judge incorrectly calculated the value of the assets. Many legal experts have said that if the error in this calculation is corrected, the judgment will change again.”

The Karnataka Government and our Kazhagam's General Secretary, the Professor, appealed to the Supreme Court, pointing out the addition error in the Karnataka High Court judgment and seeking cancellation of a decision based on incorrect arithmetic. That appeal is now pending.

The appeal initially came before Supreme Court Justices Pinaki Chandra Ghose and R.K. Agrawal. They directed Jayalalithaa and the other three respondents to file replies. The Karnataka Government and the Kazhagam General Secretary also filed responses to Jayalalithaa's petition. The Supreme Court ordered that the next arguments would take place on 8 January.

In this situation, a notice about the hearing published on the Supreme Court website on 7 January 2016 said that Justice R.K. Agrawal had been replaced by Justice Amitava Roy. The replacement of a judge in such an important case after its hearing had commenced became a subject of intense discussion among lawyers and the media.

It was reportedly discussed among lawyers that Justice R.K. Agrawal had served as Chief Justice of the Madras High Court, had earned a reputation for integrity, and was replaced because he wished to recuse himself from this case.

Nor was the change of judge the only development. On 6 January 2016, the Supreme Court issued an “Elimination List.” Reports said that because many important cases were pending before the Supreme Court and judges faced an increased workload, the Registry had stated that the disproportionate-assets appeal against Jayalalithaa would not be heard on 8 January.
But following that news, on 7 January the Supreme Court issued a “Supplementary List for Friday the 8th January, 2016,” adding Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets appeal back to the hearing list.

Why did all these things happen in this way? Why was a case listed for hearing on the 8th removed? Why was it then restored on the same day? At first, it was announced that two specified Supreme Court judges would hear the case, and the hearing began. Why, then, was one judge suddenly replaced?

Looking at all this, one naturally feels some confusion. Even so, all who believe in justice retain faith that the Supreme Court, the highest seat of legal principle, must have taken these steps lawfully for some reason, and that it will certainly permit no interference from any quarter in the processes of law.
With affection,
M.K.
10 January 2016
